source.explanation: Third-person female pronoun. Sounds the same as 'he'.
Used to point to things close to you.
Used to point to things further away.
Question word to ask for a specific item.
Equates nouns (I am a student). Never used with adjectives.

source.meaning: to be at, to be doing
source.explanation: Indicates location, or an ongoing action (like '-ing').
Indicates movement away from the speaker.
Used for reading books, watching TV, looking at things.
Used for music, listening to people.
Used for speaking languages or saying things.
Expresses fondness for things or activities.
Stronger than like. Used for people and deep passions.
Used primarily when giving your name.
Also used after country names for nationality (e.g., American person).
Often used as a respectful title to address a teacher.
Used to ask or tell someone's given name.
Refers to a specific dish of food or veggies.
Used for your residence or family members.
Time word, usually placed before the verb.
Very common. Also used to agree to things ('Okay').
Usually links nouns to adjectives (She 'hen' tall).
Negates past actions or the verb 'to have'.
Turns a statement into a Yes/No question.
Acts like 's or 'of'. My book = Wo de shu.
Shows an action is finished, or a situation changed.
A learned ability (I can swim) or future intent.
Placed before verbs to mean 'we all' or 'they both'.
The most universal counter for nouns.
Used to count bound objects like books/magazines.

source.explanation: Third-person female pronoun. Sounds the same as 'he'.
Used to point to things close to you.
Used to point to things further away.
Question word to ask for a specific item.
Equates nouns (I am a student). Never used with adjectives.

source.meaning: to be at, to be doing
source.explanation: Indicates location, or an ongoing action (like '-ing').
Indicates movement away from the speaker.
Used for reading books, watching TV, looking at things.
Used for music, listening to people.
Used for speaking languages or saying things.
Expresses fondness for things or activities.
Stronger than like. Used for people and deep passions.
Used primarily when giving your name.
Also used after country names for nationality (e.g., American person).
Often used as a respectful title to address a teacher.
Used to ask or tell someone's given name.
Refers to a specific dish of food or veggies.
Used for your residence or family members.
Time word, usually placed before the verb.
Very common. Also used to agree to things ('Okay').
Usually links nouns to adjectives (She 'hen' tall).
Negates past actions or the verb 'to have'.
Turns a statement into a Yes/No question.
Acts like 's or 'of'. My book = Wo de shu.
Shows an action is finished, or a situation changed.
A learned ability (I can swim) or future intent.
Placed before verbs to mean 'we all' or 'they both'.
The most universal counter for nouns.
Used to count bound objects like books/magazines.
